data.description:
Introduction

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies which have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. In this weekly series, I highlight recent and upcoming dividend related activity for dividend stocks on the Dividend Champions list.

In the data presented below, Yield is forward annualized and Years reflects the up-to-date streak, including dividends declared since the last edition of the Dividend Champions list.

data.title: Wheels Up: Rebound Potential With New Memberships Unveiled
data.description:
As we exit the Q2 earnings season, it has become apparent that investors are pushing back against the high valuations in the stock market. In my view, this is actually a time to take a smart "risk-on" attitude by investing in lesser-known, underappreciated small- and mid-cap stocks. By positioning ourselves this way, we're trading a bit of valuation and momentum risk for fundamental risk, which I think is the right move to make when the S&P 500 is hovering above a >20x P/E with a huge concentration in large-cap tech stocks.

Wheels Up (NYSE:UP), in particular, is a stock that has quietly outperformed the S&P 500 this year, after narrowly dodging de-listing status at the New York Stock Exchange for briefly dipping below $1 per share. In reality, this private aviation company has signaled a path back to growth with a new membership plan, improving flight profitability, and healthy liquidity, thanks in part to its partnership with Delta Airlines (DAL). Up ~30% since January, I think Wheels Up still has further room to rally.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Wheels Up in June, when the stock was trading near $1.50 per share. Since then, Wheels Up has soared ~60%, but in my view, this is just the start of a longer rebound as the company unveils a new, simplified membership plan that still offers flexibility for different types of private travelers. Amid further evidence that the company is boosting its fleet utilization and stabilizing its loss profile, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this stock.

The new Signature membership

Let's first discuss the most pertinent recent news on Wheels Up: its new simplified Signature Membership, which aims to draw in a new batch of pre-committed travelers after seeing a reduction in the company's charter members over the past few years.

The most significant feature of the Signature membership is the upfront commitment of $200,000. This works as a deposit and can be used against future flights aboard the company's fleet of Phenom 300 and Challenger 300 aircraft. But perhaps most importantly, there is a flexible mix-and-match available as well. This deposit can also be credited toward flights on Delta Airlines.

Members have a choice between two rate plans: dynamic and fixed access plans, continuing the customizable rate plans that prior members enjoyed.

Rates are shown above. Signature members who sign up for the Fixed plan get a tiered rate structure based on how much in credits they have deposited into their flight accounts. For example, a private flier with a $1 million balance can get an $8,695 hourly rate on the Phenom 300, which is a 6.5% savings against a person who has the minimum $200k balance.

A Dynamic member, meanwhile, is subject to variable pricing based on route demand. That being said, there are also larger incentives for depositing more funds into the plan. A $1 million balance, for example, gives 8% savings against the dynamic rate of the day.

To me, the refreshed Signature membership offers two key benefits for Wheels Up. First, it modernizes and simplifies the membership experience, allowing Wheels Up to go to market with a simpler sell and potentially land more members. Second, the incentive tiers on the deposit structure encourage wealthy members to deposit more cash, which potentially acts as significant, interest-free financing for Wheels Up as it looks to streamline operations and modernize its fleet.

Bookings stabilization and better fleet utilization

The new membership offer just launched in September, so we'll have to wait to see the impact on Wheels Up's financials. Meanwhile, however, there are already positive metrics that we can point to.

As shown in the chart above, Wheels Up has succeeded in stabilizing its demand, with gross bookings roughly flat at -1% y/y to $262 million in the most recent quarter. Most importantly, however, its Utility metric improved 10% y/y to 41.1 (which, as a reminder, measures the average hourly usage per aircraft within a month). The company's newer Phenom and Challenger aircraft, which are on offer in the new Signature memberships, are outperforming the older aircraft in Utility, at 49 hours and 54 hours per month, respectively.

Per CEO George Mathison's remarks in the Q2 shareholder letter:

The newer, premium Phenom and Challenger aircraft currently have the highest reliability in our controlled fleet. That, combined with the customer demand profile for these aircraft, is resulting in significantly higher Utility for our Phenom and Challenger aircraft versus our controlled fleet average, even in the early ramp-up phase of building these fleets. We expect further improvements in Utility as we scale the fleets and reduce the headwinds and inefficiencies that came from our conscious decision to accelerate paint and interior improvements into seasonally slower travel periods. Over 30% of our premium jets now feature our updated branded livery and interior, with more on the way. I am also pleased that we have continued to deliver solid operational performance as we navigate the complexity of our fleet transition. We expect our operational reliability to improve as we continue to transition the fleet.

Higher utility is also resulting in higher flight contribution margins (a measure of profitability that excludes corporate overhead and is useful for judging operational efficiency). Adjusted contribution margins saw 440 bps of y/y improvement, to 12.2%.

Of course, the key question that has been on investors' minds as Wheels Up executes its turnaround plan is, does the company have sufficient liquidity as it undergoes a gradual improvement in bookings and fleet utilization?

The good news is that Wheels Up has $107 million of cash on its books (though that's saddled by $422 million of debt), thanks to its financing partnership with Delta. This cash balance represents roughly ~1 year of adjusted EBITDA losses, based on the company's ~$85 million of negative adjusted EBITDA over the trailing 12 months.

But recent moves should improve liquidity and bolster Wheels Up's position to continue modernizing its fleet. In late August, the company divested some non-core services holdings to generate ~$20 million in additional liquidity (not included on the June balance sheet). We note as well that new Signature Members will bring at least $0.2 million in deposits at a minimum each, which should help to expand Wheels Up's cushion as well.

Risks and key takeaways

There are, of course, many risks that investors should consider before buying Wheels Up. Corporate travel is in a shaky spot as companies look to slash costs, which could reduce a key customer segment for Wheels Up (especially as the company doubles down on its strategy to pre-sell flight blocks to companies to boost utilization). We're also hoping the new Signature membership will spark renewed interest in the company and precipitate a return to bookings growth, which is far from certain.

That being said, with margins improving and a laser-focused management team that is keen to appeal to new members, I'd give Wheels Up the benefit of the doubt, especially when the rest of the stock market is expensive. Don't splurge on a large position here, but this is worth a small bet.

data.title: SOXL: Far More Risk Than Reward Since COVID Shock
data.description:
Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:SOXL) should have been one of the top ETF performers in recent years, with its ultra-leveraged design on one of the strongest rising sectors on Wall Street. However, the flaw and excuse for severe underperformance has to do with basic math and how compounding works.

When large drawdowns in price hit any investment security, the raw math in percentage returns to recover major losses becomes quite overwhelming in the real world. It doesn't matter if you own the hottest sector on Wall Street or something worse for total returns. The math is the math. Warren Buffett's Rule #1 in investing is to not lose money, because compounding in recovery mode can be similar to climbing a mountain without tools and gear.

SOXL provides a great example of why leveraged ETFs are usually best avoided in your portfolio. My view is that 3X products, either bull or bear, will eventually end up as regrets for wealth creation (if held over a number of years).

I have suggested readers avoid SOXL for a couple of years running. Since my last article in September 2024 here, this security has lost another -22.8% in value vs. respectable S&P 500 gains around +15% (including dividends).

And, believe it or not, despite a huge rally in semiconductor names since the 2020 COVID pandemic showed up, SOXL is sitting only marginally higher than it was six years ago. Three distinct downturns in the sector (including February-April 2020) have decimated this ETF's chance at compounding in line with simple semiconductor investments.

Let me explain my logic for a continued Sell/Avoid rating on the SOXL ETF for a 12-month outlook.

ETF Holdings

SOXL is the 3X bull product with a close but opposite performance relative to the Direxion Daily Semiconductor 3X Bear ETF (SOXS). Both aim to create 300% of the daily price change leverage on the NYSE Semiconductor Index (ICESEMIT), a rules-based, modified float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that tracks the performance of the 30 largest U.S.-listed semiconductor companies (according to SOXL's website description here).

The leverage for both is created by owning swaps with the largest NYC money center banks and brokerages as counterparties (too-big-to-fail institutions essentially). Swaps involve premiums and discounts (as the case may be in daily trading) that end up costing SOXL holders some capital each day. In addition, 0.75% annually is expensed to manage the fund, while some interest income is earned on sizable cash holdings (used as collateral for the swaps).

The top 10 holdings for SOXL are listed below, as of September 1st, 2025. The main individual company exposure (almost 30% of the index) is focused on Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Texas Instruments (TXN).

The website includes a discussion and disclosure that no guarantees are made that 3X daily performance will be achievable, while long-term gains/losses become less predictable as a function of the inherent leverage and costs over time.

Performance Review

If you owned SOXL during the low-volatility, rather steady increase in semiconductor stock values between January 2016 and January 2020 (just before the pandemic), you could have earned a spectacular gain, pushing +2,000% over four years, dramatically better than 1X (normal) ETF advances from the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) at +261% and plain vanilla Big Tech Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) at +140% for total returns including dividends.

Yet, this stretch proved very atypical for returns if you review semiconductor sector volatility and cyclicality. For example, since January 2020, SOXL has been a rather material underperformer vs. 1X designs. Over the past 68 months, a 3X buy-and-hold decision would have netted a minor +42% for your brokerage account. Conversely, SOXX at +205% and QQQ at +175% have earned dramatically more, employing significantly less investment risk.

That's not all the bad news for long-term holders of this leveraged-long ETF. Had you been unlucky enough to purchase your position at other cycle tops in the computer-chip sector, such as late 2021 and July of last year, your relative returns would have been even worse (in terms of lagging by a greater degree compressed over a shorter time span).

Your -64% loss on investment in SOXL from early January 2022 to the present was a disaster compared to the 1X products gaining +36% and +44%, respectively.

Almost the same relative returns over materially less time invested are evident in the snapshot below, taken from July 2024 to today. Over 14 months, -63% for a total return was a total disaster vs. the small -7% loss achieved by SOXX and +13% gain outlined by QQQ.

In summary, to achieve long-term rewards in this 3X leveraged product, steady gains are mathematically necessary, with limited drawdowns and volatility over time in the underlying semiconductor equities. Unfortunately, the stock market is known for its volatility, while major bear markets in the highly cyclical semiconductor industry historically hit every 2-3 years (measured from the early 1980s).

Final Thoughts

After failing to rally enough to recover the losses endured between July 2024 and April 2025, any downturn in the U.S. stock market could send SOXL back under $10 per share. This outcome would give nearly everyone purchasing this ETF over the past six years a sizable percentage loss on investment.

If the AI-hyped chip sector succumbs to a recession in GDP output, with falling orders, sales, and income levels, SOXL may still prove a security to avoid for another 6-12 months. I know such a scenario seems unlikely during the depths of a boom in chip-investor sentiment. However, cycle downturns do happen, often with little warning.

On the daily trading chart drawn below, you will notice how poorly overall SOXL performance has tracked vs. the S&P 500 index (lagging by -65%) over the latest 18-month period.

Price is trying to remain above its 200-day moving average, but any failure to do so may signal that another round of chip-sector selling is underway. I have also circled in gold the momentum peak for On Balance Volume reached in May. I would grade the current SOXL chart pattern and related momentum indicators as mixed, at best.

To me, the risks inherent in this leveraged-long ETF far outweigh potential rewards, especially near a Big Tech peak in pricing. I have been discussing the high odds of a technology bust in many of my articles over the past month. You can review my bearish take on ProShares UltraPro 3X QQQ (TQQQ) written several weeks ago here. In many respects, the same logic to avoid SOXL is equivalent to my bearishness on TQQQ.

I continue to rate SOXL a Sell. The smartest time to consider owning it is after a multi-month decline (at a minimum) in semiconductor names. After the super-strong run since April (rising over +200% into August's top), I cannot find a concrete reason to own it. You have to believe the Big Tech bubble will continue expanding to amazing heights as a foundational argument to be bullish. I am clearly not in this camp, having traded various investment booms/busts since 1986.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Diageo: The Lesson That Can Be Learned From Tobacco Companies
data.description:
If you're a Diageo (NYSE:DEO) shareholder, chances are your investment is in the red, with shares back at 2012 levels and 50% below their all-time highs.

Why Investing In Diageo Hasn't Worked

Many of us read about the success legendary investors like Buffett and others made by holding staple companies like Coca-Cola (KO) for a long time, and we'd all love to replicate their winnings by investing in those super tangible businesses.

Sadly, for us, the majority of staple businesses today are very far from their glory days, when growth was a matter of geographic expansion and brand awareness. Diageo is no exception. As is, a company like Diageo lacks most of the characteristics of a market-beating investment. Indeed, Diageo and other staple giants have been major laggards over the past decade:

Diageo simply doesn't have the growth prospects because it is already anywhere and everywhere. It has also squeezed every bit of juice out of pricing power during the pandemic. Further, Diageo's expansive operations have limited room for efficiency and consistent margin gains.

And, while Diageo's inherent upside is limited, it's also facing multiple headwinds and risks that don't seem to go anywhere, including rising costs due to inflation and sharp declines in alcohol consumption.

On top of all that, I called out long ago that the high barriers to entry into the industry have sharply eased in the era of direct-to-consumer and outsourced manufacturing. Nowadays, it seems every celebrity has their alcohol brand.

So, Diageo has to share a smaller pie (or one that's barely growing) with more competitors, and it cannot offset its smaller piece with higher profits, given the limits on margin expansion.

That, my friends, is typically a clear stay-away situation as investors.

Valuation Took A Long Time To Catch Up

Generally speaking, I belong to the camp that believes that if a business is struggling, you simply shouldn't invest in it, regardless of valuation. That said, it certainly doesn't help that Diageo and many of its peers came to this undeniable down cycle with lofty valuations.

In late 2021, Diageo traded above 30 times earnings, and it wasn't until 2024 that shares finally dropped below the market average valuation.

The absence of growth, a struggling business, and a lofty valuation all converged to yield the poor results. That's all important to understand as we look for something that will change that going forward.

The Business Continued To Struggle In H1

Diageo is perceived by many investors as a safe and predictable business. I argued for a long time that investors will eventually stop assigning mid-20x multiples on Diageo and many of its peers, and now it finally happened. With such businesses, though, investors are always on the lookout for a sign of change. Unfortunately, I'm not seeing it.

Sales in fiscal 25, which ended in June, were down 0.1% on a reported basis and up 1.7% on an organic basis, which excludes FX impacts, divestitures, and acquisitions. Growth was driven by a 0.9% increase in organic volume and a 0.8% rise in price/mix.

EPS dropped 39% Y/Y, or 8.6%, on an adjusted basis, as a minor improvement in gross margin was more than offset by higher operating expenses.

Looking at historic organic growth across divisions, we can see that even in segments where Diageo grew, it came from the lapping of an even sharper decline in the previous period, meaning that, except for Africa, all regions are still below their F24 levels.

Looking ahead to fiscal 2026, Diageo is expecting another year of flat sales on an organic basis (which likely means a decline in reported sales). Organic profit is expected to grow mid-single-digit, but that will come in the back half of the year.

On free cash flow, it expects a $250 million improvement, which reflects high-single-digit growth, but that is almost entirely due to lower capex.

All in all, the worst is maybe over, but that doesn't mean that what's ahead is good enough. I believe that tariffs are a new and valid excuse, but I don't think investors should just overlook them. At the end of the day, the most likely outcome for next year is no growth and flat profits, which is in line with current consensus estimates.

Valuation & The Tobacco Lesson

Going back to what I said before, I encourage investors to avoid investing in struggling businesses, regardless of valuation. Sometimes I get the response that I should look at how well tobacco companies performed when everybody said they were dying businesses.

Indeed, I think that there are some similarities to draw between the tobacco and alcohol industries, specifically that both aren't growth industries and that they are viewed in some way as "sins."

However, there are several vital nuances. First, tobacco is a consolidated industry, where competition is only declining. Second, the leading tobacco companies developed new products that are growing rapidly. Three, all tobacco companies traded at extremely depressed valuations before their rallies.

In fact, some of them still trade far below Diageo levels, and all of them traded below where Diageo is today until late 2024. This goes to show that Diageo is certainly not trading at depressed valuations just yet, and it has much less to lean on going forward.

In my mind, although I don't see it happening soon, I believe Diageo is a business that should trade at low-teens multiples, at best.

Conclusion

Even after a 50% fall from all-time highs, Diageo shares still trade at a valuation that doesn't reflect its lack of growth prospects, profitability woes, and increasing competition.

The alcohol industry, although it's viewed by some as comparable to tobacco, has very critical differences that make it far less attractive for investment.

As Diageo's business shows no sign of improvement, I argue investors should allocate their money elsewhere. That said, I believe the downside is somewhat limited.

data.title: Fractyl Health: One-Time Procedure To Lock-In GLP-1 Weight Loss; High Risk, High Reward
data.description:
Intro

Fractyl Health (NASDAQ:GUTS), a little-known medical device company advancing a one-time procedure that promises to cement the weight loss gains imparted by GLP-1 drugs, allowing patients to live drug-free at a new, lower metabolic set point, is gearing up for an eventful next 12 months, beginning with the results of a 45-patient study in GLP-1 discontinuation patients expected to report data in the month of September (any day now).

The device and respective procedure, named Revita, involves the hydrothermal ablation of a section of duodenal lining, a tissue which has been found to undergo changes in response to high-fat, high-sugar diets that disrupt the body’s metabolism such that it regulates to and defends a higher weight. The idea is that the post-ablation regrowth of this lining causes a resetting of the patient’s metabolic set point, potentially allowing patients to maintain their GLP-1 weight loss without staying on a drug indefinitely.

Revita is already approved in Europe as a standalone treatment for Type 2 diabetes (T2D), reporting 4-10% body weight loss and ~1.5% HbA1c reductions across 250+ patients as a standalone procedure (depending on the length of duodenal ablation), but has not really been commercialized due to the onerous requirements for achieving reimbursement in European jurisdictions, as well as, in our opinion, clear yet mediocre/uninspiring efficacy as a standalone treatment, which we also believe contributed to slow enrollment in Fractyl’s large US trial in T2D patients (the REVITALIZE trial, which was paused in January to prioritize financial resources for the GLP-1 discontinuation indication).

But with the advent of GLP-1s and the problem of post-GLP-1 weight rebound becoming widely recognized, Revita, if it can truly reset patients’ metabolism post-GLP-1, may have found its “killer application”. In June, Fractyl reported open-label data showing that 12 out of 13 patients maintained or saw deepened weight loss at 3 months post GLP-1 discontinuation (as opposed to 5-6% bodyweight rebound that has been demonstrated in Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk studies).

With the obvious caveats for a small sample size and open-label data in mind (the non-Revita group in the graph above is plotted from Eli Lilly’s SURMOUNT-4 trial), these early results begin to support the metabolic reset potential of Revita. The FDA also recognized this potential, awarding Revita Breakthrough Device Designation for GLP-1 discontinuation in July 2024.

Fractyl is now conducting a 350+ patient trial in GLP-1 discontinuation called REMAIN-1, which, notably, was able to fully enroll all patients between 3Q24 and 2Q25, in stark contrast to the company’s previous REVITALIZE T2D trial which struggled to enroll a similar number of patients in over three years. The first 45-patient double-blind placebo-controlled cohort from this trial is set to report data in September.

If the imminently expected data is positive—which would look like Revita patients’ weight staying relatively stable (less than 1% body weight increase would be ideal) while placebo patients regain 5-6% of their body weight—I (and perhaps the market) would start to see Revita as a unique, one-time, orthogonal addition to the massive GLP-1-driven T2D and obesity space.

Additionally, if the data is positive, the company will be set up to build momentum throughout a slew of additional readouts from its multiple ongoing patient cohorts expected over the next 12 months, culminating with the pivotal 6-month data from its 315-patient cohort expected in the early part of the 2H26.

Fractyl recently raised $23 million, which, due to its high ~$20 million per quarter burn rate, only gives it a cash runway through the 1Q26, though this will include the 6-month data readout from the same 45-patient cohort (and longer-duration open-label data). While this is a relatively short runway, the deal also included an additional $23 million tranche of warrants that are callable following the September data release, which should extend the company’s runway to(wards) the pivotal readout in early 2H26, after which the company plans to file a PMA with the FDA also in 2H26. We note that the company will likely have to raise additional capital around mid-2026, which could be an overhang on the stock if the data are not sufficiently exciting.

At a post-financing market cap of less than $70 million, and with extremely low trading volume over the past few weeks despite the upcoming readout, we think the market is overlooking Fractyl. While Revita’s lacklusterness as a standalone treatment in T2D/obesity initially gave us some pause, the encouraging early open-label data (on top of data in diabetes patients) suggests Revita has real promise as the only product in development for solidifying GLP-1 improvements. While the data due in the coming weeks is probably unlikely to cause a dramatic move in the stock price, it could get Fractyl on the radar screen of more investors and drive a gradual re-rating to a significantly higher valuation.

Weight Regain on GLP-1s

Many that follow medicine/biotech are aware of the rebound effect that occurs after GLP-1 discontinuation. In some sense, GLP-1s are such amazing drugs that losing weight is no longer the challenge, but rather being able to keep that weight off.

There have been a few large studies measuring the GLP-1 discontinuation rebound effect. The SURMOUNT-4 trial treated 670 patients with tirzepatide for 36 weeks, during which they lost an average of 21% of their bodyweight, then took half of the patients off of tirzepatide and observed them for 52 weeks. Over that time, the discontinued patients gained back 55% of the weight that they had lost (equivalent to a 14% bodyweight gain), while patients remaining on tirzepatide continued to lose weight.

Notably, about half of the weight regain occurred within the first 16 weeks post-discontinuation, similar to the 12-week period that Fractyl will be reporting data for this month.

The results were similar in Novo Nordisk’s STEP-1 extension trial, which showed that patients who initially lost an average of 17% of their total body weight over 68 weeks on semaglutide regained 64% of the lost weight over the next 52 weeks after discontinuing the drug.

Again, you can see about half of all the rebound weight is regained in the first 12 weeks post-discontinuation. This rapid weight regain shortly after GLP-1 discontinuation is consistent with Fractyl management’s commentary and anecdotes from many patients saying that they feel incredibly, ravenously hungry and experience constant “food noise” after discontinuation.

This weight rebound effect has a number of consequences:

  1. Patients lose weight progress, get demoralized, return to a less healthy state, etc.

  2. Insurers wasted money only to have patient regain weight

  3. If patients want to maintain the weight loss by staying on GLP-1s indefinitely, insurance companies effectively pay an annuity to GLP-1 companies, with no plan for getting off.

  4. Some patients are afraid to go on a GLP-1 in the first place for fear they won’t be able to come off.

The fact that the most aggressive weight rebound is seen in the 12-16 weeks post GLP-1 discontinuation makes Revita’s September readout a key incremental data point in our minds.

Historical Revita Data

Beginning in 2013 with the company’s first-in-human trial, Revita has been trialed and published data in over 250 patients to date, the large majority of which were T2D patients. Below is a summary of all of the trials that Fractyl is either currently conducting or has conducted in the past in reverse chronological order:

If you look into the results of each individual trial (which you can do in prior 10-Ks), you can see that the results range, with weight loss ranging from 3% to 10% (with essentially 100% of weight loss occurring within 3 months of the procedure) and HbA1c reduction aggregating around 1-1.5 percentage points.

The data essentially ranges from solid to mediocre, and is affected by different patient cohorts across the trials and, the company states, the fact that greater effects are seen with larger ablation areas. The company was only ablating up to nine cm of duodenal tissue in its first couple trials, while the REMAIN-1 trial ablates 16 cm.

Given that the German Real-World Registry study uses a similarly-long ablation length to REMAIN-1, it is interesting to consider the data from the 17 patients that had reached one year of follow up as of early 2025:

These patients lost 6.6% of their bodyweight by Month 3 and sustained the weight loss to Month 12, and reported a 1.0% HbA1c reduction at Month 3 that deepened further over time.

Zooming in further on the German Registry data, in August 2025, the company released data from the first 9 patients that had reached 2 years post-procedure.

These patients lost 9.6% of their body weight and saw an impressive 2.6% HbA1c reduction 2 years after being treated with Revita. You will notice that patients gained weight from 3 Months to 2 Years, which isn’t ideal, though we note that weight remained stable from 1 Year to 2 Years post-procedure.

While efficacy varies across the different studies, Revita clearly has at least some activity in metabolic diseases. Perhaps the most interesting aspect of this data as it pertains to Revita’s prospects in GLP-1 discontinuation, though, is that the weight loss and HbA1c reductions are seen rapidly (the first observation period, usually three months) and, critically, in the large majority of the data, appear to be durable out to 1 or even 2 years. So while the absolute weight loss delivered in these trials in T2D patients is not overly impressive, the data is congruent with the thesis that Revita could drive a durable metabolic reset.

Open-Label GLP-1 Discontinuation Data

In June, Fractyl gave the first glimpse of data for Revita in GLP-1 discontinuation, announcing 3-month results from 13 GLP-1 discontinuation patients that showed a median weight regain of 0.46%. Here are the detailed data:

As you can see, there is one patient that had the expected 5-6% of bodyweight regain and another two patients that gained back 3%+ of their body weight at Month 3. Other than that, most patients performed quite well, with an impressive 6 of the 13 patients losing additional weight post-GLP-1 discontinuation and 9 of 13 patients regaining less than 2% of their bodyweight or better. We also note that 6 of the 13 patients had an improving effect from Month 1 to Month 3, indicating that the benefits of Revita may take a couple of months to fully manifest.

Here is the data plotted against the 5-6% total bodyweight gain from Lilly’s SURMOUNT-4 trial:

It is also worth noting that Revita’s stellar tolerability profile was maintained in the open-label results:

The ability to offer patients a one-time, pain-free, nausea-free solution to getting off of their GLP-1 would likely be a key selling point for potential patients.

While these small, open-label results are extremely preliminary, they give some early indication that Revita may have the ability to effectively “lock-in” most of the weight loss gains of GLP-1s, a much more impressive and socioeconomically valuable effect than its results in T2D.

September Data: The Bar for Success

While the readout expected this month is only 3-month data in 45 patients, Revita’s rapid onset of action and the rapidity of post-GLP-1 weight rebound should provide plenty of opportunity for Revita to show significant separation. Management has commented that its goal is to deliver 50% better results than what would be expected from tirzepatide withdrawal normally (i.e. 2-3% bodyweight regain vs. 5-6% expected).

Obviously this is a conservative goal versus Revita’s open-label data, but it gives some leeway for natural variance, and signals that management believes the study is a success even if the Revita arm of the trial doesn’t fully mirror the open-label result. While we believe a 50% improvement versus placebo would still be a technical and clinical success, we are not sure the investment community would be thrilled.

Given the limited open-label data, we would conservatively hope for bodyweight regain of around 1% or better at 12 weeks (vs. the ~5% expected for placebo), which we believe would be considered a success by both the scientific and investment communities. If the data were to be in the 2-3% bodyweight regain at 12 weeks, Revita would likely still be a viable medical device candidate, though less exciting, and possibly of significantly less interest to investors.

Overall, it is difficult to predict what the market considers to be the line between success and failure in this trial (assuming there are people watching this trial closely at all). In order to really get on the radar screen of investors that are not currently aware of the company, we believe the results likely need to be in the 1% bodyweight regain or better range.

Next 12 Months

Following this month’s results, Fractyl has a series of other readouts from the three different cohorts expected in the next 12 months, including:

  • 6-month data from the open-label patients in the 4Q25

  • 6-month data from the 45 placebo-controlled patients in the 1Q26

  • 1-year data from the open-label trial in 2Q26

  • 6-month data from the 315+ pivotal cohort

Over the next 12 months, Revita will be pretty comprehensively characterized as a treatment for GLP-1 discontinuation patients, culminating in a relatively near-term opportunity to submit a PMA in the 2H26. The upcoming 12-week results serve as a proof-of-concept, setting both the expectations and the level of investor interest/excitement for these upcoming readouts.

Market and Commercialization

To put some numbers to the market size and opportunity for Revita in GLP-1 discontinuation patients:

  • There are 100 million people with obesity in the US

  • There are ~10 million patients on GLP-1s

  • More than 50% of patients will discontinue GLP-1 therapy within 3 months

  • 85% of GLP-1 discontinuers will regain weight

  • More than 250k bariatric surgeries (significantly more invasive than Revita) are performed each year

  • There are more than 20 million endoscopies performed each year in the US, including over 600k advanced endoscopic procedures

  • Of the ~10 million patients on GLP-1s, ~800k will get an endoscopy for various reasons

Revita is a ~45-minute outpatient procedure (with little/no side effects) that Fractyl states can be easily performed by advanced endoscopists at an estimated 2,000 to 4,000 sites in the US today. It takes just a couple of patient walkthroughs for physicians to become proficient in the procedure, and there are already 32 clinical sites performing Revita procedures as part of the REMAIN-1 trial, on top of the 51 US sites that were performing Revita procedures as part of the paused REVITALIZE trial in T2D (though many of these sites overlap). The company also signed a letter of intent with Bariendo in June to potentially partner on providing Revita through its network of endoscopic obesity treatment centers.

Fractyl also has the interesting option of potentially positioning Revita as an add-on procedure to the 800k GLP-1 patients that will undergo an endoscopy each year. The kicker here is that, according to the company, most gastroenterologists/anesthesiologists already require that patients stop GLP-1 medicine prior to an endoscopy due to the slowed gastric emptying. This could be a natural “GLP-1 off-ramp” offered to patients that allows Revita to seamlessly penetrate its market opportunity.

On the payor side, in an interview with Inside Precision Medicine, CEO Harith Rajagopalan stated that he thinks payors are desperate to be able to deprescribe GLP-1s, which makes intuitive sense since many patients will either discontinue (rendering the money essentially wasted) or continue to use GLP-1s indefinitely (a GLP-1 annuity) with no plan of how to get off and cement the health gains. Fractyl has indicated that early internal discussions surrounding Revita’s pricing are settling around the equivalent of 18 months of GLP-1 therapy ($500-$1,000 per month), which, if the durability of Revita can be proven to insurers, should be a welcome long-term solution.

Recent Financing

Fractyl recently completed a $23 million equity offering that also includes two additional $23 million warrant tranches (with exercise prices of $1.05 per share), one of which is callable following the data expected this month if Fractyl’s trading price exceeds $1.37/share.

Fractyl guided that the $23 million will allow it to get through the release of 6-month data from the 45-patient REMAIN-1 cohort in the 1Q26, and we would expect the runway to extend to the full 315-patient 6 months results in early 2H26 if the first tranche of $23 million warrants is able to be called and exercised (though that would require the stock gaining some momentum driven by the upcoming results).

The named investors in the financing included healthcare-focused investors Nantahala, ADAR1, and SilverArc, as well as Second Line Capital and 683 Capital. ADAR1 was a 5% owner of Abivax (ABVX) going into the August data readout that saw the stock soar 600%+, not that that means anything about its investment in Fractyl.

Fractyl’s cash situation is definitely tight, and puts extra pressure on the demonstration of Revita’s value over the next couple quarters. If data is less than stellar, the capital overhang could be a serious problem for the stock.

Risks/Questions to Ask

Revita’s Long, Winding Road

Revita began its first trial in T2D in 2013 and received its CE mark in Europe in 2016, but never commercialized in Europe to any meaningful extent because of the challenging regulatory and reimbursement statuses of the individual countries. It did gain reimbursement status in Germany in 2022, after which it began a pilot commercialization effort and its German Real-World Registry trial, both of which gained some minimal traction before being paused as part of the reprioritization towards GLP-1 discontinuation in January 2025. The company also decided to pause the enrollment of its primarily US-based REVITALIZE trial in T2D after enrolling for over three years. While the shift to GLP-1 discontinuation is clearly the right strategic decision, Revita’s long history and lack of real success in T2D makes it seem like an unlikely blockbuster medical device candidate at first blush.

Unproven Efficacy

There is very limited data available for Revita in GLP-1 discontinuation patients at present. Beyond the small 13-patient open-label data, we are using Revita’s results in T2D patients to infer about GLP-1 discontinuation patients. The durability of Revita’s weight maintenance in post-GLP-1 patients, which will be crucial to its viability as a treatment, also won’t be known for at least a couple more quarters.

Open-Label Data

While the open-label data looks incrementally positive, and the company includes a detailed breakdown of the data in its slide deck, we find it a bit odd that Fractyl released the 3-month open-label data from only the first 13 patients in the open-label study, rather than waiting for the full 22 patients enrolled in the study to reach 3 months.

Data Readout Pushbacks

Though it could probably be blamed partially on financial constraints, Fractyl does have a history of pushing back the expected readouts of its trials. For example, it originally announced that it would report the open-label REMAIN-1 results in the 4Q24 (it came in June of 2025), and the REVITALIZE trial (now paused) was also supposed to report results in 2024. That said, with all cohorts of REMAIN-1 already fully enrolled as of May 2025, the guided timeline over the next few quarters should be accurate.

Oral GLP-1s

While we don’t necessarily think the oral GLP-1 drugs currently in development represent a substantial threat to Revita, primarily because of their side effect profiles and that patients/physicians/insurers will still want a long-term solution, there could be more convenient, less nauseating oral drugs developed that at least some portion of patients would likely opt for over a procedure like Revita.

Digital Health Results from Omada Health

We are aware of a small, in our view partially flawed though still interesting study conducted by Omada Health that found that its digital health program was able to essentially eliminate weight regain in patients discontinuing their GLP-1 medication. If these results were to be replicated, it could represent an alternative to an invasive procedure like Revita, though we note that a large number of patients will not be either physiologically or psychologically capable of preventing weight regain through behavior alone.

Fear of Endoscopic Procedure

While it is essentially equivalent to a routine endoscopy, some patients will never consider Revita simply because it involves anesthesia.

Updated GLP-1 Practices

While there will still always be a number of patients that abruptly stop GLP-1s for financial, clinical, or other reasons, the idea of very slowly titrating down a GLP-1 instead of just discontinuing treatment will likely continue to become more and more prevalent, potentially improving GLP-1 discontinuation outcomes.

Dilution Risk

As we discussed in the financing section, there is potential dilution from the $23 million of warrants that could be exercised if Fractyl’s stock is trading above $1.37 after this month's data, which would only get the company to the 2H26. A worse outcome would be that the stock price never gets to $1.37 and the company needs to do a raise at sub-$1 prices.

Conclusion

Fractyl sits at a critical juncture: It has a lot of interesting yet incomplete data to suggest that its Revita procedure could be a viable metabolic reset treatment to cement GLP-1 weight loss, and is expected to report the first of a series of important data readouts that will characterize Revita’s ability to address this large opportunity imminently. While the company’s sub-$70 million market cap as it approaches its first placebo-controlled readout in this large and novel indication has caused me to question if I am missing something, I have looked under pretty much every stone I could find (with the results conveyed in this piece), and the recent investment from respectable healthcare investors lends some incremental solace. While the financing situation will continue to be an ongoing concern and will put extra pressure on the company to report exciting data, Fractyl is underfollowed and potentially just ~12 months away from being able to file a PMA in the massive and as of yet completely untapped GLP-1 discontinuation market.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers Health: At The Intersection Of Secular Megatrends
data.description:
Introduction

The share price of Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) has been extremely volatile since I recommended it as a Strong Buy in late 2024. Nevertheless, the stock delivered several massive bull runs within the last nine months and is currently 45% higher than it was when the initial recommendation was published. I am confident that this share price strength is explained by the company's stellar fundamentals and bright future prospects due to favorable secular trends. The forward P/E ratio trajectory looks extremely bullish, meaning that I continue buying with the reiterated Strong Buy recommendation.

Fundamental analysis

According to McKinsey's latest "Future of Wellness" survey, the wellness market is already an enormous $2 trillion industry. According to the study, the industry has great potential to grow further because Millennials and Gen Z are making wellness a daily, personalized priority, not just an occasional activity.

As the following picture suggests, younger generations are likely to spend more on wellness than older generations do, which is certainly a tailwind for the industry.

This survey from McKinsey is also very important while analyzing the potential of HIMS because it also reveals the most important wellness areas for younger generations. According to the survey, "sexual health and skin and hair care needs are more important to GenZers and millennials than to older generations." This trend is favorable for HIMS because the company excels in these areas.

What we know from common sense is that younger generations are also much more digitally savvy, which is a big tailwind for the digital health market. According to Grand View Research, the global digital health market is a thriving industry that is expected to observe a 22.2% CAGR by 2030.

With all that being said, it is obvious that HIMS is firmly positioned at this intersection of these secular megatrends driven by the changing preferences of younger demographic groups. The company is extremely successful in capitalizing on these tailwinds, which is apparent if we look at the TTM revenue curve. The latest reportable quarter saw a staggering 72.6% year-over-year revenue growth.

Another beautiful chart from HIMS is the company's TTM EBITDA growth. As we can see, the metric's growth repeats revenue, indicating solid potential to convert revenue growth into profits. Therefore, it is not surprising that Wall Street analysts expect HIMS to expand its EPS from $0.60 in FY2025 to $3.02 by FY2032, a massive five-fold increase.

Prudent capital allocation from the management is an important indication that they prioritize quality growth for shareholders. The company's outstanding share count has been relatively stable over the last several quarters, which is quite rare for an aggressively growing business. The total debt has increased from almost zero to one billion dollars during Q2, but it is still ten times lower than the company's market cap.

I also like that the management prioritizes investing in niches that are demonstrating aggressive growth and vast opportunities to capitalize on them. For example, HIMS demonstrates success in a very promising weight loss niche, which is particularly promising in the U.S., where obesity is a problem across even younger age groups. HIMS does not have enough scale to compete with leaders like Novo Nordisk or Eli Lilly in terms of R&D invested in GLP-1 drugs. However, it does not mean that HIMS does not have its competitive advantages. The company differentiates itself by providing a more personalized and holistic program for its customers with 24/7 team access, which pharmaceutical giants are highly likely to find too large to replicate.

Besides expanding into promising markets, HIMS also has endeavors in driving revenue growth by expanding internationally. The company made some bold moves in this respect during the summer of 2025. HIMS announced that it will start its expansion to Canada in 2026, once the company completes the acquisition of ZAVA, which is expected to help in its European expansion.

Let me finalize my analysis of fundamentals by adding a little bit of technical analysis here. The RSI indicator of HIMS has increased from 35.78 (almost oversold) to 51 within just two days, which might indicate that the share price trend is reversing because the performance of the HIMS share price over the last three months was a bit disappointing.

Valuation analysis

The forecasted forward P/E ratio trajectory of HIMS looks absolutely bullish because the multiple is expected to shrink massively between FY2025 and FY2028. The projected EPS growth looks realistic considering the ability of HIMS to capitalize on favorable secular trends.

Bullish long-term EPS growth outlook from Wall Street is backed by the projected aggressive revenue growth rate. The company's forward P/S ratio sits at 4.3 at the moment, which is just slightly above the sector median of 4. This gap might be unfairly narrow because HIMS demonstrates unparalleled growth across all key financial metrics compared to peers.

Wall Street analysts forecast FY2027 revenue to be $3.3 billion. Multiplying this value by the sector median P/S ratio of 4 means that the fair market cap by the end of FY2026 is around $13.2 billion. This means that HIMS has a 26% upside potential (compared to the current market cap of $10.5 billion) over the next 16 months, which looks compelling. Therefore, I think that HIMS is very attractive at the moment.

Mitigating factors

The healthcare sector is strictly governed, and any modifications in rules could impact not only the organization’s ongoing activities but also its expansion objectives. Failure to deliver on expansion initiatives could result in a slower-than-anticipated sales increase or reduced operational efficiency.

Any successful sector draws in additional new competitors, particularly those with minimal financial hurdles to entry. Relatively low investment needs are reflected in the company’s cash flow report.

The stock price increased by 229% over the last twelve months, which is highly likely to tempt some investors to bank profits. HIMS is an extremely volatile stock, with the share price range over the last month spanning between $13.5 and almost $73.

Conclusion

HIMS remains a compelling buying opportunity due to its rock-solid fundamentals and the bright future of the entire wellness industry. The stock still offers compelling upside over the next 16 months, which certainly outweighs potential risks and uncertainties.

data.title: YTD Asset Class Performance Through August 2025
data.description:
Until this year, US stocks had consistently been the best-performing asset class over the past 15 years, so diversification did not "work." But this year, diversification outside the US into foreign stocks has added value, and gold is shining very brightly.

Consequently, diversified target date funds are winning this year. Most TDFs are concentrated in US stocks and bonds.

Diversification

Here's a comparison of TDF glidepaths, highlighting the greater diversification in the SMART TDF Index versus the S&P TDF Index.

Now What

And so it's beginning. US stocks are becoming less attractive as they become even more expensive and other asset classes like gold and foreign stocks take the lead. Several articles have documented the fact that the US stock market is currently very expensive, so in a "bubble" that is likely to burst as it has in previous Minsky Moments. Your reaction to this bubble threat is driven by several factors that determine your risk tolerance, like your age, investment horizon, and your gut. It's much more than "Do I feel lucky today?"

Fear and greed are powerful motivators. Warren Buffet advises “be fearful when others are greedy and to be greedy only when others are fearful.” Bubbles keep expanding when others are greedy. Young people with a long horizon can be greedy because they can ride out the next correction, but older people have a much shorter horizon and less risk capacity.

As I've written previously in articles like "Revenge of the Baby Boomers" – that was read by more than 80,000 SA readers – older people near retirement should consider moving to safety while they are currently in the Retirement Risk Zone that spans the five years before and after retirement when they are exposed to Sequence of Return Risk.

How are you reacting? Is there a bubble?

Conclusion

The benefit of diversification is better risk-adjusted returns, but we usually see unadjusted returns, so diversification appears to have been unrewarded in the previous 15 years, but now we see the benefit this year even in unadjusted returns.

data.title: UMB Financial: Fundamentals Increasingly Priced In (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of UMB Financial (NASDAQ:UMBF) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 20%. With the company successfully beginning to integrate Heartland Financial after its acquisition, we are seeing strong financial results, pushing the stock towards a 52-week high. Since I last covered shares in May, UMB Financial has gained 23%, outpacing the market’s 15% rally and exceeding even my own expectations. With Fed rate cuts appearing imminent amidst a slowing labor market, now is a good time to see if UMBF has further upside.

UMB Financial is a mid-sized regional bank with about $72 billion of assets. It primarily operates in the central and Southwest portions of the United States with a network of nearly 200 branches. Earlier this year, UMB Financial completed its acquisition of Heartland Financial, greatly improving its scale. Still, the company remains solidly below the $100 billion asset threshold, providing it meaningful room for further growth before facing additional regulatory costs.

Thus far, the integration appears to be on track. In the company’s second-quarter earnings, reported on July 29, UMB Financial earned $2.82 per share, which beat estimates by $0.78. As you can see below, operating earnings more than doubled from last year to $225 million. Operating EPS was up a more modest 37%, given the increase in the share count for the Heartland purchase. Still, this deal is already proving to be substantially accretive, even with the higher share count.

Given the acquisition, year-over-year comparisons have limited utility, as essentially every metric is up substantially. However, sequential results speak to how well the company is performing even amidst integration. This picture continues to be encouraging. Deposits were up nearly 67% from last year to $60 billion. Again, I think looking at sequential trends is more useful. They grew by nearly $1.5 billion, or 10% annualized, a very strong pace. UMB has a very resilient deposit base; 46% of deposits have been with the bank over 10 years. A healthy 26% of deposits are noninterest-bearing (“NIB”), reducing funding costs. UMBF had a 3.44% cost of interest-bearing deposits, flat to last quarter, and I expect to see declines in H2 given Fed rate cuts.

On the other side of its balance sheet, loans were $36.8 billion, up 50% from a year ago. Sequentially, loans grew by about $870 million, or a 10% annualized pace. Heartland’s balance sheet was less loan-intensive than UMBF’s, and I expect UMB to work to grow loans more quickly than deposits to reduce excess liquidity and improve margins. On the earnings call, management said the headwinds initially felt in April amid tariff uncertainty have faded away. As policy uncertainty declines, we should see lending growth improve further, in my view.

In terms of loan quality, UMBF has a diversified loan book. Still, about 29% of the loan portfolio is in investment commercial real estate. This sector has faced more challenges, but UMB’s underwriting is strong. The average loan-to-value is just 57%, and 85% of the portfolio has recourse, both of which should greatly limit losses. Additionally, just 4% of the total portfolio is in office, and with more employers bringing workers back into the office, this exposure is less problematic than even a few months ago.

UMB has a history of strong underwriting, and that continues to be the case. As you can see below, its delinquencies are lower than a year ago at 8 bps. Bringing in Heartland, we have seen a jump in delinquencies, but even here, there was a very encouraging improvement sequentially. As we see UMB apply its more stringent underwriting standards over the next year, I expect consolidated delinquencies to improve. Meanwhile, UMB is carrying $390 million of allowances, up by $21 million. This provides about 4x coverage of its $97 million in nonperforming loans (NPLs), well above my 2.5x threshold, meaning credit performance could get worse before UMB really needs to add more to reserves.

UMB also has a $17 billion securities portfolio, largely of fixed-income securities bought when interest rates were lower. As such, it has a ~$450 million unrealized loss on the portfolio. As bonds mature and get either reinvested in new bonds or used to fund loan growth, we will see the portfolio yield continue to improve. Indeed, there were $605 million of cash flows in Q2 at 3.11%; UMB reinvested at 4.63%. About $1.8 billion of securities mature over the next year. The securities portfolio has about a 5-year duration, so this will be a prolonged but steady build in interest income.

Because of this, UMB’s net interest margin [NIM] was 3.01%, but this was aided by purchase accounting following its acquisition of Heartland. Excluding this, NIM was 2.83%, up 8 bps sequentially. Net interest margin was 2.51% last year. I expect a modest further improvement in Q3. I would note that net interest income was boosted by $42 million in Q2 thanks to the accretion of loans and securities held by Heartland. Another $714 million remains to be recognized, which will occur over the next few years. We see at least $180 million of this being realized by the end of next year. To the extent that loans are paid off early, we could see more of an impact.

One other item to consider about the forward outlook is that the company did have a $29 million gain from its stake in Voyager Technologies (VOYG), which IPOed in the quarter. It has ~904,000 shares. Given where shares are trading today, we will see a ~$12 million loss as shares have pulled back from their post-IPO highs, meaning Q3 will likely be sequentially weaker than Q2. I would expect UMBF to exit this investment by the end of next year, though it has no commitment to do so. UMB has several investments via its private investment team, and with valuations rising and the IPO market improving, there may be other potential gains.

Importantly, UMB remains a well-capitalized bank. It has a 10.4% common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio, which is 9.5% CET1 adjusting for AOCI. Given it has less than $100 billion in assets, it does not need to include AOCI losses in its calculation, but this is still relevant when considering its economic capital position. I expect to see capital continue to build and potentially reach 10% inclusive of AOCI within 12 months, which could then enable buybacks alongside its secure 1.3% dividend yield.

Now, my expectation is for the Federal Reserve to cut rates two times this year and at least two times next year. However, if the economic outlook weakens further, we could see a faster pace of rate cuts. Because most of its loan book is floating rate (as is the case for most regional banks), rate cuts are a headwind to net interest income. As you can see below, rate cuts reduce UMB’s potential net interest income, with a larger impact over time, as it takes some time for loans to reset (some loans reset monthly, others quarterly, etc.). If we see the Fed cut rates more aggressively, that does pose a downside risk to earnings, though it would also reduce the bank’s AOCI loss.

Back in May, I saw UMBF having about $10.50 in 2025 EPS with the potential to grow to $11 next year as it has a full year of Heartland contributing. I still see UMBF earning $10.50, +/- $0.10 this year. Given my assumption of 4-5 rate cuts over the next year, I still see the bank positioned for at least $11.00 of EPS next year. In this market environment, I view ~9-12x as the fair multiple for regional banks, depending on their growth and capital positioning. Given tailwinds from growing its loan book and its solid loan quality, I believe UMBF should trade in the upper tier or about 11x forward earnings.

This suggests ~$121 is fair value for shares, which is near where shares are trading today. Particularly with the potential for more rate cuts, I see less upside in shares of UMBF, and I see momentum slowing in the stock going forward. Still, given its solid fundamentals and trading close to fair value, I do not see a need to sell shares. I am downgrading shares to a “Hold” and see them as performing in line with the market. If there was a pullback below $115, I would consider buying, but for now there is no rush to add shares.

data.title: OneConnect: The New Business Model
data.description:
OneConnect Financial Technology (NYSE:OCFT) stock is a hold, as it has a very high risk in its restructuring execution, with high cash burn and a lack of profitability while still operating at a loss. The next quarters will be essential for the company's fate, and if the stabilization of its margins will happen, with its indicators becoming positive.

The Company

Is a Technology as a Service (TaaS) provider, operating mostly in China for financial institutions with a growing ambition for global expansion. The company’s main shareholder, Ping An Group (OTCPK:PNGAY), is also one of its largest clients, and this association offers positive and negative points for OCFT, in which on one hand it gains credibility in the market, however, on the other, it creates dependence on PNGAY, becoming evident when the revenue from Ping An and Lufax fell by 59%, directly affecting the company's total revenue.

OneConnect went through a restructuring where it eliminated 99.6% of its cloud platform revenue as an extreme change, representing an implicit admission that the previous business model was unsustainable, as its very high operating expenses led to low profit margins. After this restructuring, it focused on 3 main segments:

  • Digital Banking, which offers solutions for retail banks, digital credit management, and core banking systems, and despite being the main focus of its new business, it showed a decline in the first half of this year due to macroeconomic issues, since its compensation is made by transactions, and with the slowdown in the economy, banks reduced credit granting.

  • Digital Insurance, a segment for solutions for the auto, health, and life insurance sectors, becoming a positive point for the company, growing by 11.2%, indicating that among the three, it is the most promising, being the only one that did not show a decline.

  • Gamma Platform, a platform that offers a basis for solutions, being the epicenter of the restructuring, and despite showing a decline like Digital Banking, it was a planned and direct decline to focus on other more profitable areas.

H1 25 Results

At the end of August, the company reported its results for the first half of this year under its new model. Its revenue from continuing operations was approximately $112 million, a 43.4% decrease compared with last year, and this drop was primarily driven by the deliberate phasing out of its low-margin cloud services segment whose revenue fell 99.6%. The company's gross margin compressed to 26.1% from 37.1% in the same period last year, reflecting the loss of scale. The Digital Insurance segment grew 11.2%, demonstrating strength in the company's new strategic focus area.

Sector

OneConnect operates in the Fintech as a Service ((FaaS)) market, which at the moment, is undergoing rapid expansion and growth. On a global scale, this market was valued at $417 billion in the year 2025, with growth rates between 14% to 16.3% until 2035, being expected to pass $1 trillion. In the Asia-Pacific, China is the main driver of this market which in 2024 was valued at $4.59 trillion, with projections for its value to double by 2030, reaching almost $10 trillion.

The trends of this market in the country mostly involve the digitalization of the sector, as its economy is considered cashless, being directly driven by the presence of payment apps that create a base for active consumers in the digital environment, generating a demand for digital financial services, and with the integration of AI and big data being applied for credit enhancement and fraud detection, improving the efficiency for these consumers.

OCFT is well-positioned to capture these trends, with its insurtech segment being of higher growth in the latest results, being able to double this number if it focuses on this trend, along with its AI and big data tools, which have easy integration and can thus be contracted by small to medium-sized banks, to specifically obtain the fraud detection tool, reducing the implementation cost.

Competition

This market is large, therefore, the competition within it is fierce, having large companies that are already strong and established. The leaders are financial and digital companies, such as Alibaba and Tencent, which have millions of users on their platforms, which are used daily by these consumers, therefore, the company's strategy of selling its technologies to banks and insurers is an attempt to position itself as a facilitator, with its success depending entirely on the willingness of these institutions to invest in competitive technologies against the already consolidated companies.

The company's expansion into the international market comes not only from a growth opportunity but from a need to diversify its geographic and regulatory risk from China, where the government increases regulatory issues and the local economy slows, leaving companies with an exclusively Chinese market more vulnerable and dependent.

Valuation

Revenue

The company generates revenues through the B2B model, offering services to companies in the financial niche, such as banks, insurers, asset managers, etc. Its revenue streams come from fees, separated into Implementation, generated from the construction and installation of its technology, and Transaction, from processed transactions, monthly subscriptions, and technical or maintenance support, these being recurring revenue services.

The projection for Transaction Revenue is negatively impacted by the failure in the expansion strategy and the continuous reduction in spending by client companies, and with that, a decline will be projected for its segments, even with the cloud segment reaching zero, because the portfolio cleanup in this scenario was not offset by new business, thus resulting in a lower revenue than in the previous period, showing a loss of -29.3%, reaching $153.4 million. The projection for Implementation Revenue is made from a waterfall format table, where the result of one quarter is used as the basis for the next, in which each period a minimal and insufficient revenue of $1.8 million from new contractors is added, along with the loss of -$0.5 regarding the conclusion of old projects, this being a continuous negative impact, also leaving the revenue lower than in the year 2024, reaching only $83.3 million.

Starting from 2022 the scenario changed, and revenues began to show continuous drops motivated by the macroeconomic and regulatory scenario in China, which became increasingly difficult, thus directly impacting OneConnect's clients. With this economy growing at a slow pace, there was lower demand in credit granting and other financial activities, in addition to the reduction of its clients' budgets, who due to pressure on its profitability, had to cut or postpone projects that involved investments in technologies, thus resulting in less revenue.

For the projection of the last quarters of 2025, the scenario continues to show a drop in revenue, assuming that the company's recovery strategy did not work as expected, considering that it operates at a loss and do not generate profits. In Q3 the implementation revenue falls to $19.9 million, reflecting the difficulty in closing new international contracts and a greater termination of projects in China, with the transaction revenue also showing a drop to $39.1 million, motivated by the reduction in client spending, and the sum of the segments totals a revenue of $59.0 million, a drop of -2.48% compared to Q2. For Q4, the seasonal improvement will not happen, considering that the implementation revenue will continue to show drops, falling to $19.5 million, with the transaction client base also decreasing, leading the segment's revenue to $38.7 million, resulting in a total revenue of $58.2 million.

For 2026, the pessimistic projection shows an acceleration in this decline, with the failure of international expansion becoming evident, where the first quarter with the lack of new international contracts will lead the implementation revenue to fall to $19 million, accompanied by the transaction segment which will decrease to $38.1 million, due to continuous pressure on clients, with the total result showing a drop of -1.89%. In Q2 the downward trend increases, thus causing the implementation to reach $18.6 million, and the transaction revenue to continue decreasing to $37.3 million, resulting in a drop of -2.1%.

EBITDA

Its EBITDA is currently at -$23.13, this being a clear sign of OCFT's inefficiency in generating profits, indicating that the profit from its services is not enough to cover the most basic operating costs and expenses, even before considering debt interest, depreciation, or taxes. In the long run, a negative EBITDA places great pressure on the company's management, so that its new business model not only generates revenue but is also profitable.

Historically, the EBITDA has always been negative, a characteristic present since it became public. The company always focused on accelerated growth, investing heavily in R&D, sales, and marketing, and this approach kept it at an operating loss for many years, which forced it to cut costs, and eventually, start its restructuring since the growth at any cost was not sustainable.

EV/EBITDA

Because its EV/EBITDA is negative at -3.26x, the multiple to be used to calculate the stock's price target in 2026 is its P/S, currently at 1.16x. This number is a reflection of the pessimism and the uncertainties surrounding the company's, considering that the other companies in the FaaS sector trade this multiple between 5x to 10x, which make its current value is extremely low. Several factors can justify this value, such as the reflection of its revenue collapse in recent years, and with projections pointing to declines, the market has priced it in advance in relation to this. The absence of profitability is also a relevant factor, because in addition to operating at a loss, it also burns cash, leaving its P/S as one of the few applicable metrics.

Using this metric and the revenue projection for the next 12 months of $230.2 million, its stock and its Market Cap will show a decrease of 5.86%, with the price per share starting from $7.21 currently, reaching $6.78, a decline value already presented in previous moments, and the Market Cap starting from the current value of $283.62 million, decreasing to $267 million.

Net Income

Operating at a net loss is also a characteristic of the company, demonstrating a significant net loss with the difficult periods it has been facing. Currently, this number is -$94.5, larger than the previous year, not being the reflection of just one bad quarter, but an accumulation of all its problems, with low margins and the collapse in revenue. Despite this, the losses in the first quarter of 2025 were significantly smaller than the second half of 2024. Because it is consistently negative, the path to the company's profitability depends entirely on its operating leverage, in which after many cost cuts, its expense base is smaller, thus needing only consistent revenues that will bring better gross margins.

Drivers and Risks

In my point of view, the most relevant growth driver for OCFT is its expansion into the international scope, this being the pillar of its current business. The company has already guaranteed its presence in places like Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Africa, counting on providing services to 214 financial institutions, in 20 different countries, with some concrete contracts such as Old Mutual in South Africa, and CIMB in the Philippines. The growth of revenues from clients outside of China would be the sign that this model is working, being able to increase not only the company's revenues but also its profitability, which would consequently make its margins and multiples rise.

The most prominent risk at this moment for OneConnect is its dependence on the Ping An Group, because as previously mentioned, the drop in its revenue and from Lufax is the most imminent risk. Any other deterioration in this relation that is fundamental for the company will have an impact on its revenue, leaving it vulnerable to contract renegotiations as it has low negotiating power. This strong association with Ping An can also make sales to the group's direct competitors difficult, who may see the company as an arm of a rival rather than as an independent supplier company.

Although its net cash position is a long-term mitigator, it is not infinite, therefore, the company operating at a loss and with operational cash burn is a financial and operational risk. With the continuation of this loss the reserve will continue to be consumed, raising the question of whether the company will be able to reach a break-even point before this cash runs out.

Conclusion

The company at the moment has high risks, such as the absence of profitability, operating at a loss and burning a lot of cash, and its lack of proof regarding its recovery, and it cannot be considered a sell option because it still has financial security, such as the fact that its cash is greater than its debt, for example. At the moment, the best is to hold the stock, and wait for more evidence of a real improvement from the company.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Kraken Robotics, VICI Properties, AeroVironment And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today we're looking at articles published between Aug. 29 and Sep. 4.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

QDTE Is Another High-Yield Champion, At Least For The Past Year

Damon Judd | Buy

As we begin to approach what appears to be another potential top in the market in September 2025, there remains plenty of interest in high yield income vehicles from retail investors. While I hold many income investments in my own Income Compounder portfolio (between 30 and 40 holdings), it is the higher yielding, riskier ones that seem to capture the most interest from readers on this site. Case in point is my recent review of YMAX vs. YMAG, which has gotten above-average page views and more than 90 comments as of today, 9/3/25. Conversely, my review of the safer, lower-yielding review of the DNP Select Income Fund (DNP) has received less than half the page views and has about 30 comments so far.

This time around I want to take a look at the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (BATS:QDTE), and compare results with other similar high-yield income funds that write covered calls against tech stocks. One thing that is unique about QDTE compared to some of its peers is that the fund pays weekly distributions, while most of its peers pay out monthly (except YMAG, which also pays weekly).

VICI Properties: I'm Backing Up The Truck Despite Las Vegas Tourism Slump

Pacifica Yield | Buy

VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI) has built a one-of-a-kind portfolio of high-quality gaming and entertainment destination assets, subject to long-term triple-net leases that provide for annual base rent escalations. This has set the backdrop for sustained adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") growth and a healthy dividend yield. I'm bullish but flagged risks from the Las Vegas tourism slump when I last covered the ticker. VICI last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.4325 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.73 per share annualized for a 5.18% dividend yield. The REIT last raised its dividend by 4% in September 2024 and has compounded its distributions by 7.77% annually since 2020. This is set to continue, with VICI being able to lean on multiple levers for growth.

Nextracker: Valuation And The End Of Political Drama Suggest Taking Profits

Vince Martin | Hold

Fundamentally, Nextracker (NASDAQ:NXT) stock looks ridiculously cheap. Even with a 65% rally year-to-date, shares trade at a little over 16x guidance for fiscal 2026 (ending March) adjusted earnings per share. Given clear leadership in solar trackers, impressive growth (adjusted EPS rose 38% last year, though there's a bit of a politically driven pause on that front this year), and solar's long-term outlook, that multiple looks almost impossibly low.

That's because the multiple is somewhat deflated: The Section 45X tax credit is boosting profit margins currently, though that benefit will start to fade in a few years. Adjusted for 45X, the multiples assigned to Nextracker stock look much more reasonable – and perhaps high enough to incorporate even impressive growth through the end of the decade. Given relief around the industry and the impact of the "One Big Beautiful Bill" signed in July, there is a case here that NXT at this point is reasonably priced.

Indeed, the consensus sell-side estimate now projects just 7% appreciation over the next 12 months. And it's not terribly difficult to see why. This is an excellent business – one perhaps worth owning longer-term – but valuation simply is much more aggressive than a first glance suggests. At this point, there do seem to be better options elsewhere in the space, and at the very least, real questions about near-term catalysts for Nextracker stock.
